speaker: interviewer
text: Action. OK, Myra, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is September the 5th, 1996. We are in Las Vegas, Nevada. My name is Myra Slotnick, and it is my pleasure to be interviewing Harold Blitzer.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, are you ready? OK, Myra, any time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please tell me your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Harold, middle initial M, Blitzer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was Heinz, first name was Heinz, H-E-A-I-N-Z, and middle initial was Martin, and last name Blitzer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born August 9, 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Berlin, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your family life in Berlin before 1933.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before 1933 I remember we had a store. And my father was in the poultry business. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- I was only seven years old so not too much I remember. We had ice cream in the summertime. And we were known to make the best goose liver pate in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who lived in your house with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name was--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was Marie, or Mania was-- was the Polish name or Jewish name. And she was born Webber.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Leo, or Levick, Blitzer. No middle initial.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a sister. Her name was Lilli, and she was four years older than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me-- tell me about your school life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, initially when I was six, I went to a German school. And in 1936 we moved-- it was getting tough to do business. My father sold the store. And we moved to another apartment. And I went to another school there for about a year or so. But at that time, the teachers came in uniform, and the kids made-- beat me up during-- during rest periods. And so it was finally decided to put me into a Jewish school. And the school was-- it was sort of strictly a boys school. It was at the Grose Hamburger Strasse, which also had the name of the school, you know. And it was considered like a middle school almost, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going on about-- I was 10, about 10. I stayed there. My father opened up another store, which I thought was a big mistake. And-- and we stayed there for about two years at that address. And until about 1938, right before the Crystal Night he sold the store. Or he got out of it. I don't know if, at that time, he could sell it. I wasn't that into. And we moved to another apartment. And in 1938, Jews had a tough time to get streetcar permits, and-- and the only way I could attend the-- the-- my school was by street car. So my father transferred-- my mother transferred me to a Jewish school about two or three city blocks from our apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you learn in that school? What kind of subjects? Well, we learned-- we learned everything more or less, you know, like typical grammar school and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] arithmetic and history, of course. I loved history. And-- and I-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Matter of fact, we had one year of English, I remember. And the only thing I remember about was we used to start each English session with I said to my neighbor, good morning, neighbor. And that's the only thing I ever knew and retained from my English session. And I loved to play soccer, but at that time, it was very difficult. The Jewish community only had one place. And many times when I was scheduled-- a number of times when I was scheduled to play, we got out to the soccer stadium or the soccer place, and there was a sign up by order of some kind of a Nazi official that the place was closed for the day. So I-- I was very fortunate if I got-- I got to play maybe once every two or three months. And we had no-- at the school I was, we had no-- we had a very small gymnasium, and, you know, we had no place to play. We had no-- no place to play soccer or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so we were at that time already very closed in, you know, till November when the famous Crystal Night started, when they-- when they burned down our synagogue and our-- our school was right at the same place. So I remember I came-- I went with my friend over-- I met my friend at the school. We saw the school burning and were sort of a little happy there for a while. You know, I said, my god, we don't have to get up in the morning anymore, right? But that didn't last too long because then you started realizing what are you going to do? And so he asked me, he said, let's go over to see-- go over to my father's store. He's got-- he's cleaning up all the windows were broken. We help him. I said, why not. This was 8 o'clock in the morning. Where was I going to go? So we went over the-- his father's store. We helped him cleaning up. And all of a sudden, we saw that big mob coming towards the store. And the-- the father locked himself and ourselves into the repair shop, which, thank god, had a metal door.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was no other way to get out. That's it. The only way you can get out-- go back to the store. So we were locked in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were locked in about six or seven hours. We heard the people. Who has size eight? And they were handing out school-- shoes to everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [DRINKS] and so around 3 or 4 o'clock, there was quiet. And he opened the door. And the only thing we were basically worried about was what happened if they set the store on fire, you know. And we would have been-- there was no way for us to get out because if we would have opened the door, they would have killed us. That's the kind of mood there were-- these people were in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans. Average German people. All of a sudden, they could see, like, my god, we get shoes for nothing, you know. This was a very fine store. He made shoes to order. He was a-- they were good-- women's shoes mainly, you know. Good quality and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once we got out, the whole store was empty. He didn't-- he just went home with his son, and I went home. And my mother and my father said where have you been, you know. And we were worried.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, well, I told them the story. We were locked in for about six hours in that store. And I wasn't supposed to go on the street for the next couple of days. There wasn't-- and I didn't. And now, we started realizing-- I started realizing what it is to be without school. All of a sudden, I had no place to go. I had no place to even talk to somebody. We were not allowed on a-- play somewhere because-- so it was kind of sad, you know. And also in 1938, my sister was picked up on a street in the famous Poland action. And she was deported because my father and my mother was Polish, therefore, she was considered Polish. My father, from one reason, had completely forgot about it. But my uncle and the older son was deported who lived around the corner. And-- and thank god, she had some relative in Poland, you know, so they took care of them. She couldn't-- you know, she and her girlfriend couldn't speak a word of Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you imagine, they chased the people in the middle of night over the border. The Poles chased them back. And finally, they were going back and forth. Finally, the Germans says, you better keep those. These are your people. And so they finally let them in because the Poles did not love the Jews either. Let's be realistic about that. So she was then in Poland so--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had no business anymore. So he did-- he collected-- what do you call the-- he went to the different tailor shops. And then part of the recycling, the-- the [INAUDIBLE], which was very big at that time in Germany. And then he made sort of a living. But he lugged all those things on his shoulder. And-- and we had-- we didn't really know if we were poor or not. When you're that young, you don't know. And in 1939, believe it or not, I had a bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was amazing. I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they rebuilt the synagogue a little bit. Not as a synagogue-- this was not-- they had a big room, you know. And they put-- they saved the Torahs and things like that. So they-- they had like a-- like a make-- makeshift synagogue. The place was at the Kaiserstrase where our school was. The school, they never bothered to rebuild, you know. There was too much damage. Everything was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a bar mitzvah there. And my mother-- I don't know how she did it. She even made me a suit. But she didn't have any black material like-- the tradition was always for a kid to wear black at the bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she found some white material. So this was the first time I-- I-- [LAUGHS] the first time the rabbi said this is unusual, you know, in his speech. And I-- I remember the rabbi. His name was Levy. I got a picture of him that I found. And so she had a party at the house for around 30, 40 people. And I have no idea where she got that food from. We had-- I mean, this was like unbelievable. It's like being in heaven again with all the food, with the cakes and everything there. And-- and this was probably the last time-- I didn't know it at that time-- this was basically the last time we all were together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your sister with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, my sister was not there because she was already deported to Poland, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at that time, we were fairly left alone, personally, because my father still had a Polish passport, and he was still considered a foreign subject. And so when the war started, September 1 in 1939, he was arrested with all-- with many, many Jews, and mostly Poles. And whatever they could find, they arrested and put them in the-- in the concentration camp near Berlin called Sachsenhausen. It's also one of the first concentration camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that-- every day my mother and myself, since there was no school, we went to the Jewish community and-- with all the other women and wives and daughters and whatever-- and trying to find out-- we didn't know where my father was. We didn't know at that time how to get him out. But that's when we went to the Jewish community to find out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at one of those-- we went there every day, walking, because we were not allowed to use the street car. And one lady, downstairs one day-- I am-- it seems like I remember it exactly what happened, today-- said to my mother, she says they're making up a list of 300 children to go to Denmark. Why don't you put your son's name on the list?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were certain requirements. You couldn't be 15 years old-- not older than 15. And you had to be, like, from 12 or up to-- it was only like a three year-- well, I-- we went upstairs. She put my name down and completely forgot about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a couple of months later-- [DRINKS] this was done sometime in September or October. I don't know the exact day then. All of a sudden, about December or January was, we were notified we were accepted, to get all the papers together.

speaker: interviewee
text: I needed permission to leave the country. I needed all kinds of papers. And we did it. We walked around to all these different places, including the police department and the Gestapo. They had to give permission. That was the secret police they had there. But it wasn't as secret as-- as the name said, you know. They were-- they were all around. And so by-- we were-- I was scheduled to leave on February the 6th, 1940. Lo and behold, my father gets released on sometime the last week of January.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out of the-- he was in a place called Sachsenhausen. He was released with the understanding that within 30 days, he leaves Germany. At that time, Jews were still allowed to leave. And some had places where they could go to. And so my father at that time went to the Jewish center, and he was assigned on the next boat-- those were illegal transports going to Israel to break the blockade. At that time, the English still had a blockade. The Jewish organization had steam-- had riverboats which went-- some of them amusement or freighters and things like that-- went on the Danube, all the way through Hungary or Czechoslovakia, all the way to Romania to the Black Sea. And supposedly at that time, some big ship was going to pick them up. Well, the winter of '39, '40 was one of the coldest winter almost in the history of Europe. The Danube was frozen. So my father couldn't-- couldn't leave. He was supposed to immediately for Vienna to get put in the transport there. And-- and then something amazing happened. At that time, also like about the end of January, my sister pops up. What happened was her and her girlfriend met some German soldiers there and told them a story like we were tourists. We lost all our papers. And we are from Berlin. And you know, all they spoke was German without an accent and everything. And they'd like to get back to Germany. And this guy says, well, my truck is going. Go ahead. Jump on the truck. And he took them all the way to the house. And that's how she popped in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was also the last time when we were together. On the 6th of February, the-- everybody took me to the train station. And everybody was crying except me. To me, this was a great adventure. 13 years old, what do you expect?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and there was a bunch of kids there with me. And one kid I knew very well. And we were in school together and everything. And we thought this was fun, you know. And we got on the train, and we said our goodbye. And my mother was crying. And-- and never realizing this was the last time I would ever see my sister and my mother again. So we went on a train, and I wound up in Denmark.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened to your mother and sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I only found out about it-- I--I knew they were no longer alive after the war because the first thing you do, you put-- I remember after liberation, you put your names down. You want to look for your family and things like that. So for a number of years, you had some kind of a hope, you know. But I only recently, last year, the tracing service of the Red Cross notified me because of a request-- every so many years we put a request in. And then one time a couple of years ago, we were notified due to the east Europe is now open. A lot of the records are available because the Germans kept records of everybody. And these records are available. And you want to find out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was notified last year that my sister was evacuated-- that's what the letter reads-- evacuated in-- in March of 1943. And there was no word, but I assume my mother was among the same people because both my sister and my mother worked at a place called the Siemens factories, which I know have a very prominent position in the United States today. It's an electronic company.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I don't have any Siemens razor in my house. [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE] So-- so I-- I was kind of upset about the wording of this. So I wrote Mrs. Dole who was the president of the Red Cross at that time a letter and objected to this kind of-- you know, if you find out these things, why don't you tell it as it is. They were not evacuated. They were deported, you know, and-- to a-- to a death camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got a nice letter back from some lady who was told by Mrs. Dole to answer me. And the explanation was that's the wording the German Red Cross used. And they must apologize I took it so hard and things like that. But we-- we-- we did exactly what the German wrote. We just put it into English. So, well, what are you going to do? So that's what I found out. My sister was officially deported in March of 1943, yeah. Tell me what happened-- tell me the circumstances under-- under which you went-- well, when you went to Denmark, what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you with? Tell me the name of the friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Henry. Henry Kahan, K-A-H-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't know what we were going to do in Denmark. We thought, like, we were going to go to school or something like that. But we had a wakening up after about we spent a couple of days in Copenhagen. And we saw all the-- the-- we saw the beautiful stores with all the Danish pastry, which, by the way, in Denmark it's not called Danish pastry. It's called Vienna bread. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was about 30 or 40. But some of them had arrived earlier and were still coming. And so we were put up in a-- in a-- in a pension for a couple of days-- one of those [INAUDIBLE] you know, with breakfast. And we met every day. And then they gave us the orientation-- what we were going to do. And they said we are now being assigned to-- to-- each person will be assigned to a farmer on different islands. You know Denmark is an island community. And so we were assigned to a place called Fyn, which was the middle island.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell that for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: F-Y-N. And it was pronounced 'fern.' And this is the island where, actually, Hans Christian Andersen was born. He was born in the city of Odense, which was at that time only like 20 miles from where I was. We landed, believe it or not-- the winter was so-- so strong that year at-- the ferries-- Denmark had ferry condition between the main island and all the different islands. But even that part of-- was frozen up. So-- [DRINKS] for the first time in my life, we were sent-- we-- we took a plane. This was an official, Scandinavian airline system plane with one engine. And-- [LAUGHS] and I think it had like eight or ten, twelve passengers. And we boarded the plane, and again this was big excitement. I'd never been on a plane.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- [LAUGHS] and we went there. It took like an hour and a half or something at most to get where we were going to go. We landed in Nyborg, and we were assigned to a-- to a family, right? And again, [LAUGHS] we were supposed to get schooling, right? And then we were told once a week in the afternoon from 1 o'clock till 5 o'clock, we had a teacher. And he was going to give us schooling, right? In the meantime, the minute we got to this farmer, we got up at 6 o'clock in the morning milking cows and doing all the things we never-- believe it or not, there was nights when I cried like a baby because I looked at my hands and it was-- I thought, especially, it was so cold that time. I wasn't used to that kind of thing. After all, 13 years-- what do you expect, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so this once a week, we were looking forward to. I had a bicycle. They got-- we got a bicycle. And-- and, by the way, we never get paid. We weren't supposed to get paid. That was the deal. But we figured out that's-- you know, you get used to it. You know, it was like we were safe. And two months later, April 9, the Germans marched into Denmark. So they were following me again. So being on a farm away from the cities, we never saw any German soldiers. But we knew they were there because all of a sudden, all products-- you know, like pigs and milk and cheese-- was now delivered to actually to the German government because-- which, to them, Denmark was a heaven because all of a sudden all that export now was going-- It got so bad that the--the--the kids in the cities were-- were-- the people in the cities were highly rationed. This is a country which produces an abundance of food, you know. And all of a sudden, the-- the kids in-- in school, the kids in a city like Copenhagen or Odense or wherever the big cities was had to be like every summer during their vacation there, farmers took in two or three kids so they can feed those kids. That's how-- I know we had three at my farm. We had three sisters from Copenhagen. I even know their name. [LAUGHS] I was so in love with one of them, you know. [LAUGHS] They came from very rich people then. The-- the one-- the older sister was called Ose. Ose. And the second sister was-- no, it was Birthe, Birthe, B-I-R-T-H-E, Hanne Jenssen. They always have like two names, you know. And I found out after the war that the-- the-- the father was a General Motors distributor. He handled Chevrolets, trucks, and Opel cars. So they were from a rich family, but they didn't have nothing to eat, you know. So every year these kids came to that farm, you know. And we-- [LAUGHS] I was just looking forward to it. Again, I was too young to appreciate it. So-- but we worked very, very hard, you know. And all of a sudden, we lost our teacher in 1941. He received his permission to go to the United States. And the only way he could go to Russia. He went to Russia. That was before the Germans' march. I think it was '40 or '41. I'm not sure the exact-- all of a sudden, we had no more school. And that meant now we were like seven days a week. And believe it or not, I went to a couple of different farms because, one, I couldn't stand because it was so hard, you know. They never gave me anything. I was treated like-- first of all, we never slept in the house. We were sleeping above the stable. And real-- no heat. No nothing. The heat from the animals. And the smell, of course. And the flies-- don't forget the flies in the summertime-- kept us warm. And not that I'm complaining about it, but I felt we should have been treated a little bit with more dignity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like, finally, at many times we used to say, well, let's get some money, you know. Let's get-- why can't we get a little pocket money? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So some places at the end, like in '42, we were getting a little pay. We were actually considered cheap labor, you know. And I'm very-- don't get me wrong, I'm very grateful for Denmark. [DRINKS] How many people were with you? How many kids were with you on those farms?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had about, I think in my group, were about 30 or 40 kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: That continually stayed together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stayed in that area. Nah, when we're talking about in my area, we're talking about one lived six kilometers away. We were-- we were all on farms, you know. And they were-- The last job I had was-- was on a farm which was almost 15 kilometers to the Nyborg, to the city where we used to have school. But at that time, I couldn't use my bicycle. The tires were shot, and we couldn't get any replacement.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was sort of locked in on that-- that farm. And-- and-- Were you with other kids on that farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I was strictly by-- by yourself. And we were each assigned to a-- to a different place, you know. And the idea was we're supposed to be-- this was all preparation for Palestine. And we were going to go on a kibbutz. We were very pro-Israel at that time. We all wanted to go to Palestine and work. And, you know, instead of working for this guy, we were going to work for ourselves, you know. We were very idealistic, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So life goes on. Like I said, things are getting-- the-- the-- Denmark was-- was one of the greatest country for German to be an occupation because nobody got shot there. And the-- there was hardly any underground. And, you know, occasionally a newspaper came out. And you were listening to the BBC broadcast at night, which was against the law. But we were miles away from any German. Very rarely did we see any Germans. They might have come by transport on the highway. Who got over to the highway, you know? So the cities we were were little villages, you know. They had a general store. And they had like a community center. Maybe once every three months, a movie company came in and played a movie for us, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those-- you-- you lived on that farm, and you basically died on that-- you could-- I could picture myself 20 years from the day being still on that farm. In other words, there was no other job. I used to-- the idea of working seven days a week used to drive me crazy. Not that we worked all day Sunday, but we had to go and milk the-- milk the cows and clean the stables. Nothing goes on. The chickens have to be whatever, you know. All of a sudden, I became a farmer, and I hated it. I wanted-- I didn't care. Get me away from this farm and so--

speaker: interviewer
text: What changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: October 1, 1943.
